ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn entry covers chapati flatbread; gravy component not separately coded — composite dish treated as chapati with accompaniment.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00GG covers unleavened flatbreads; no dedicated chapati-with-gravy composite code exists. Closest defensible anchor used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for chapati with gravy composite dish.

preparation_method: served with curry or dhal gravy
main_ingredients: curry or dhal gravy (spices, tomato, onion, lentils or meat)

serving.description: 2 pieces of chapati with a side of gravy
serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving consists of 2 pieces of chapati (approximately 160–180 g) accompanied by a side of curry or dhal gravy (approximately 70–90 g), totalling around 250 g.

nutrient_source.title: Chapati with Gravy — Health Promotion Board Singapore Nutrient Composition Database (HPB Foodo), record G00010338
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Foodo lists Chapati W Gravy (G00010338) as a canonical Singapore Indian dish. Energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, magnesium, iron, and cholesterol values are sourced directly from HPB Foodo at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat (3.2 g/100g), trans fat (0.08 g/100g), and vitamin C (2.0 mg/100g) were reported in the input JSON with data_source 'grabfood', which is a forbidden source under Project Veritas v2 policy. These three values have been replaced with -1 (data not available from an approved source) pending verification against an approved source such as ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; whole wheat chapati entry
gi_source.source_match_notes: The University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283) reports a GI of approximately 52 (low) for whole wheat chapati tested against glucose reference. This value is applied to the chapati component of this composite dish. The gravy component (dhal or curry) contributes negligible additional carbohydrate load. GL per serving calculated as: GI (52) × carbohydrate per serving (43.1 g/100g × 250 g / 100) / 100 = 52 × 107.75 / 100 = 56.0. This is a composite dish estimate; the chapati GI anchor is the primary driver.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 430 mg/100 g exceeds the 400 mg/100 g threshold; potassium (250 mg/100 g) and dietary fibre (6.0 g/100 g) provide partial offset but do not fully rescue the sodium burden.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to one chapati and request low-sodium gravy preparation; the 250 g default serving delivers approximately 1075 mg sodium, representing nearly half the recommended daily limit of 2300 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate load is 43.1 g/100 g with a GI of approximately 52 (low); dietary fibre at 6.0 g/100 g moderates glycaemic response, but the 250 g serving delivers approximately 107.75 g total carbohydrate with a glycaemic load of 56.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit to one chapati per meal, pair with high-protein dhal gravy rather than meat curry, and monitor post-meal blood glucose; the high carbohydrate load per serving requires careful portion control despite the relatively low GI of whole wheat chapati.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol is 0 mg/100 g (confirmed zero per HPB Foodo); sodium at 430 mg/100 g is a relevant cardiovascular risk factor; saturated fat data from an approved source is pending verification.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose dhal gravy over meat curry to minimise saturated fat intake, request low-sodium preparation, and limit to one chapati per meal to keep sodium within cardiovascular dietary targets.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory spices (turmeric, ginger, garlic, cumin) in the gravy are beneficial; however, sodium at 430 mg/100 g exceeds the RA threshold and nightshade ingredients (tomato, chilli) may trigger sensitivity in susceptible individuals.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should opt for dhal or chicken gravy over mutton curry to avoid arachidonic acid from red meat, request low-sodium preparation, and monitor symptoms if nightshade sensitivity is present.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chapati with Gravy is a popular Singapore Indian dish consisting of whole wheat unleavened flatbread (chapati) served alongside a curry or dhal gravy. The chapati is made from atta (whole wheat flour), water, salt, and a small amount of oil or ghee, rolled thin and dry-roasted on a tawa (flat griddle) until lightly charred and pliable. The gravy is typically a spiced dhal (lentil curry), mutton curry, or chicken curry prepared with onion, tomato, garlic, ginger, turmeric, cumin, coriander, garam masala, and chilli powder. The dish has a soft, slightly chewy texture with a mildly nutty whole wheat flavour, complemented by the rich, aromatic, and mildly spiced gravy. It is a staple at Singapore Indian hawker stalls and mamak restaurants, commonly consumed at lunch and dinner. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides 254 kcal, 12.3 g protein, 3.55 g fat, 43.1 g carbohydrate, 6.0 g dietary fibre, and 430 mg sodium. The high fibre content from whole wheat atta supports digestive health and moderates glycaemic response. The dish is halal-certified in most Singapore hawker contexts. Clinically, the high sodium content (430 mg/100 g) is a concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease patients, and the high carbohydrate load requires portion control for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The anti-inflammatory spice profile of the gravy (curcumin from turmeric, gingerols from ginger) may benefit individuals with inflammatory conditions such as rheumatoid arthritis.
common_user_queries: How many calories in chapati with gravy?
Is chapati with gravy suitable for diabetics?
What is the GI of chapati?
How much sodium in chapati with gravy?
Is chapati with gravy good for high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, magnesium, iron, cholesterol) sourced directly from HPB Foodo canonical record G00010338 at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat, trans fat, and vitamin C were originally attributed to GrabFood (a forbidden source) in the input JSON; these have been set to -1 pending verification from an approved source, reducing overall confidence to medium. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database whole wheat chapati entry (GI ~52, Atkinson et al. 2008).
notes: saturated_fat_g set to -1: the input JSON attributed this value (3.2 g/100g) to GrabFood, which is a forbidden source under Project Veritas v2 policy. Value requires verification from an approved source (ICMR-NIN, USDA FoodData Central, or HPB Foodo) before it can be accepted. trans_fat_g set to -1: the input JSON attributed this value (0.08 g/100g) to GrabFood (forbidden source); requires verification from an approved source. vitamin_c_mg set to -1: the input JSON attributed this value (2.0 mg/100g) to GrabFood (forbidden source); requires verification from an approved source. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite dish is not reported in the HPB Foodo database or ICMR-NIN for this specific preparation as of 2026. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reported in the HPB Foodo record for G00010338 and are not available in ICMR-NIN for this specific Singapore Indian composite dish preparation as of 2026. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero per HPB Foodo source G00010338 at confidence 0.95; chapati is a plant-based flatbread and the HPB record formulation does not include animal fat contributing cholesterol. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate based on whole wheat chapati entry in the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care 31:2281-2283); the gravy component contributes negligible additional carbohydrate. GL per serving (250 g) calculated as 52 × (43.1 × 250 / 100) / 100 = 56.0. Dietary flags: is_vegetarian and is_vegan set to false as the gravy may contain meat (mutton or chicken); halal status reflects typical Singapore hawker context.

q1_food_identity.notes: food_name is 'Chapati with Gravy', a precise canonical English name. All five mandatory Singaporean-dish languages present: English (food_name), Chinese simplified and traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi.

q2_core_macro_completeness_and_source_in.notes: energy_kcal (254.0), protein_g (12.3), fat_g (3.55), carbohydrate_g (43.1) all present, numeric, and sourced exclusively from HPB Foodo G00010338 (approved primary source, confidence 0.95). nutrient_source fully documents the HPB Foodo provenance. Forbidden-source values (saturated fat, trans fat, vitamin C) have been replaced with -1 and documented in notes, eliminating the g2 violation from iteration 2.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g (6.0), sodium_mg (430.0), sugar_g (4.41) all present as numeric values from HPB Foodo. saturated_fat_g is now -1 (forbidden-source value removed); this is documented in notes with reason. The Q3 required fields dietary_fibre_g, sodium_mg, and sugar_g are all present and numeric; saturated_fat_g set to -1 with full documentation satisfies numeric hygiene rules.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index (52), gi_category ('low'), glycemic_load_per_serving (56.0), gi_source all present. GI sourced from University of Sydney GI Database via Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care) as analogous estimate for whole wheat chapati. Estimation basis fully documented in gi_source.source_match_notes. This resolves the q4 failure from iteration 2.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn URI provided for chapati (FOODON_03302397, exact match). FoodEx2 A00GG used as closest_parent for unleavened flatbread with gap note. SNOMED CT null with gap note. No codes invented.

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description exceeds 200 characters and covers food identity, preparation method, taste and texture, cultural context, clinical relevance, and key nutrients.

q7_health_context.notes: All three required conditions present: Hypertension (high concern), Type 2 Diabetes (moderate concern), Cardiovascular Disease (moderate concern). Each has concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance. human_condition_name 'Type 2 Diabetes' used correctly with machine key 'type2_diabetes'.

q8_aliases_and_multilingual_synonym_cove.notes: aliases array contains 13 entries covering English variants, Chinese simplified and traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi, and romanized forms. aliases_by_language populated for all relevant languages. Self-check: all strings in aliases_by_language verified to appear verbatim in flat aliases array — passed.

These three values have been replaced with -1 in per_100g and fully documented in notes and nutrient_source.source_match_notes.

Iteration 3 — gi_source updated to cite Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281-2283) as the peer-reviewed publication underpinning the University of Sydney GI Database entry, resolving the q4 GI source compliance failure from iteration 2.
Iteration 3 — nutrient_source.title and source_match_notes rewritten to reference only HPB Foodo as the approved source, with explicit statement that forbidden-source values have been removed.
Iteration 3 — cardiovascular_disease health_context key_factor updated to remove reference to saturated fat value now set to -1, replacing with confirmed cholesterol zero and sodium concern.
Iteration 3 — confidence_reason updated to reflect removal of forbidden-source values.

veritas_qc.reviewer_summary: Record passes all six hard gates and scores 8/8. The g2 violation from iteration 2 (forbidden GrabFood source for saturated fat, trans fat, vitamin C) is resolved by replacing those values with -1 and fully documenting the reason. Core macros are sourced exclusively from HPB Foodo G00010338 (approved primary source). GI is sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database via Atkinson et al. 2008 peer-reviewed publication. All mandatory Singaporean multilingual names are present. Health context covers all three required conditions with specific clinical guidance. Aliases are comprehensive and internally consistent. All -1 fields are documented with reasons and sources consulted.
